claim: Specimens were disc shaped, in the as-cast condition, approximately 7 mm in diameter and 1 mm in thickness, produced by levitation melting of high-purity materials.
evidence_text: Disc shaped specimens in the as-cast condition approximately 7 mm in diameter and 1 mm in thickness were individually oxidized... prepared from high purity materials using the technique of levitation melting.

claim: Post-oxidation characterization included surface and cross-sectional SEM, EDAX qualitative analysis, optical metallography, X-ray, and electron-probe microanalysis of polished cross-sections.
evidence_text: Specimens were then examined using the scanning electron microscope (SEM), and pertinent morphological features were qualitatively analyzed using EDAX. After surface examination... metallographically polished... for examination of the scales and alloy structures using optical, SEM, EDAX, X-ray, and electron-probe microanalysis.

claim: Few alloys obeyed simple rate equations; kinetics approaching parabolic were observed only infrequently, so weight gain after 24 h (ΔW/24) was used for comparison.
evidence_text: This indicates that few, if any, alloys obeyed simple rate equations and that kinetics approaching parabolic were observed only infrequently. Instead, the weight gains after 24 hr oxidation (ΔW/24) were used for the comparison of oxidation rates.

claim: SEM and metallographic examination revealed four distinct morphological scale types, and an oxide map (Fig. 3) was constructed by superimposing these composition regions on the 800°C isothermal phase diagram.
evidence_text: subsequent SEM and metallographic examination of mounted and etched cross-sections revealed the structure of the alloys and four distinct morphological scale types. The “oxide map” for the Fe–Mn–Al system as shown in Fig. 3 was constructed by superimposing the composition regions of each of the four morphological scale types upon the isothermal section of the phase diagram at 800°C.

claim: Region I alloys (insufficient Al for continuous alumina) exhibited alumina interspersed with large bulky oxide nodules; nodules on alloys with <7.5% Mn were predominantly iron oxides.
evidence_text: Alloys from regions I and II contained insufficient aluminum to form continuous protective alumina scales... Scale morphologies of alloys from region I consisted of alumina interspersed with large bulky oxide nodules. Nodules formed on alloys containing less than about 7.5% Mn were composed predominantly of the oxides of iron.

claim: A large oxide nodule on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al had a white outer layer (Fe2O3) and an inner layer (Fe3O4), with Mn and Al enrichment in the Fe3O4 layer and at the gas-oxide interface.
evidence_text: A typical cross-section of a large oxide nodule formed on Fe–7.5Mn–5Al (Fig. 4) revealed a structure very similar to that of nodules formed on binary Fe–Al alloys. The nodule was composed mainly of a white outer layer (Fe2O3) and an inner layer (Fe3O4). X-ray images (Fig. 5) show a high degree of enrichment of manganese and aluminum in the Fe3O4 layer and at the gas-oxide interface.

claim: A small nodule on Fe–10Mn–5Al consisted of a grey manganese oxide layer at the gas-oxide interface above a white Fe2O3 layer, with aluminum-rich phase particles and a continuous Al-rich layer at the nodule-alloy interface.
evidence_text: The structure of a similar typical small nodule (Fig. 7) consisted of a grey manganese oxide layer at the gas-oxide interface above a white layer (Fe2O3). Particles of an aluminum rich phase were present in this layer and also formed a continuous layer at the nodule-alloy interface.

claim: Region II alloys with small austenite proportions formed bulky manganese-rich oxides as linear arrays where austenite plates intersected the surface, as shown on Fe–15Mn–6Al.
evidence_text: Alloys with small proportions of austenite in the structure formed bulky manganese rich oxides, which grew as linear arrays where plates of austenite intersected the surface (Fig. 8).

claim: Areas of spalled alumina were associated with voids and bulky manganese-rich oxides (Fig. 11), and the white Fe2O3 phase progressively disappeared at alloy manganese contents above 20%.
evidence_text: Areas of spalled alumina were frequently associated with voids and bulky manganese rich oxides (Fig. 11). ...As the alloy manganese concentration increased, the white phase (Fe2O3) progressively disappeared and was no longer a scale constituent in alloys which contained more than 20% manganese.

claim: Region III exhibited very low 24 h weight gains and formed adherent protective scales with small oxide protrusions (<1 μm); protrusions were scarce at low Mn but abundant at high Mn concentrations.
evidence_text: Region III of the oxide map was characterized by very low 24 h weight gains and formation of very adherent protective scales, which were not penetrated by nodules or other bulky oxides. The scales were nearly featureless except for small oxide protrusions with dimensions generally less than a micron. ...Few protrusions were observed on low manganese alloys; however, they became abundant at high manganese concentrations (Fig. 12).

claim: Scales formed on Fe–10Mn–6Al oxidized for 100 h at 600°C consisted of alumina and numerous randomly scattered bulky and threadlike oxides.
evidence_text: Scales formed at 600°C on Fe–10Mn–6Al consisted of alumina and numerous randomly scattered bulky and threadlike oxides.

claim: At 800°C after 100 h, the scales on Fe–10Mn–6Al were similar to those after 24 h but showed degradation including lifting, buckling, and growth of small bulky oxides at localized sites (Fig. 14a).
evidence_text: At 800°C the scales were similar to those formed after 24 hr and showed signs of degradation including lifting and buckling and growth of small bulky oxides at localized sites on the surface (Fig. 14a).

claim: After 100 h at 1000°C, Fe–10Mn–6Al formed thick convoluted alumina scales that adhered to the substrate except at specimen edges, were much thicker than at 600 or 800°C, and consisted of two distinct layers (Fig. 14b).
evidence_text: At 1000°C the scales were thick convoluted alumina, which adhered to the substrate except at sites on the specimen edges (Fig. 14b). The scale was much thicker than at 600 or 800°C and consisted of two distinct layers.

claim: After 100 h at 1000°C, Fe–20Mn–8Al formed a convoluted alumina scale identical to that on Fe–10Mn–6Al except it spalled badly on cooling; the external layer was enriched in Mn and Al, and the inner layer contained very little Fe or Mn.
evidence_text: At 1000°C a convoluted alumina scale was formed. It appeared identical to that formed on Fe–10Mn–6Al at 1000°C except it spalled badly on cooling. The external scale layer was enriched in both manganese and aluminum, and the inner layer contained very little iron or manganese.

claim: Duplex alloy Fe–10Mn–4Al–5Cr formed scales identical to those on certain alloys from region II, consisting of alumina and bulky oxides directly overlying precipitated austenite.
evidence_text: Scales formed on Fe–10Mn–4Al–5Cr appeared identical to those formed on certain alloys from region II of the oxide map, consisting of alumina and bulky oxides directly overlying precipitated austenite.

claim: For Fe–5Mn–4Al–5Cr, the reported ΔW/24 of 0.292 mg cm⁻² was obtained after only 15 h oxidation; after 100 h this alloy gained 0.327 mg cm⁻². Other selected 24 h weight gains include: Fe–5Mn–5Al–5Cr 0.068, Fe–7.5Mn–4Al–5Cr 0.196, Fe–7.5Mn–5Al–3Cr 0.122, Fe–7.5Mn–5Al–5Cr 0.063, Fe–10Mn–4Al–5Cr 0.115, Fe–10Mn–4Al–12Cr 0.061, Fe–15Mn–5Al–5Cr 2.663 mg cm⁻² (Table III).
evidence_text: Table III. Compositions and Weight Gains of Fe-Mn-Al-Cr Alloys Oxidized at 800°C ...

claim: The 800°C oxide map (Fig. 3) was constructed by superimposing oxidation behavior on the isothermal phase diagram, assuming alloy phases after 24 h oxidation were similar to equilibrium and boundaries did not shift markedly; this assumption is supported by small structural changes in Fe-10Mn-6Al and Fe-20Mn-8Al after 100 h.
evidence_text: It was assumed that the alloy phases observed after 24 hr oxidation were similar in composition and proportion to the equilibrium phases and that the phase boundaries did not markedly shift with longer heat treatment times. On the basis of the small changes that were observed in the structures of the alloys Fe–10Mn–6Al and Fe–20Mn–8Al after 24 and 100 hr heat treatments … it is considered that the phase boundaries lie close to those at equilibrium.

claim: In alloys with insufficient Al to form continuous alumina, nodules on <7.5% Mn are large, composed of outer Fe2O3 and inner (Fe,Mn)3O4, with Al2O3 or aluminate particles/layers at metal-oxide interface; on >7.5% Mn nodules are small with outer continuous Mn2O3 layer, inner Fe2O3 and (Fe,Mn)3O4, and continuous healing layer of alumina/aluminate at interface.
evidence_text: Nodules formed on alloys which contained less than 7.5% manganese … composed mainly of an outer layer of Fe2O3 and an inner layer of (Fe,Mn)3O4. … Nodules formed on alloys which contained more than 7.5% manganese were much smaller … and were composed of distinct, complete external grey layers of Mn2O3 above layers of Fe2O3 and (Fe,Mn)3O4.

claim: When alumina or aluminate particles partially isolate (Fe,Mn)3O4 from substrate, outward cation diffusion is retarded but Fe2O3 growth unchanged, increasing Fe2O3/(Fe,Mn)3O4 thickness ratio; complete isolation converts nodule to Fe2O3 with little (Fe,Mn)3O4.
evidence_text: Enhanced Fe2O3 formation may occur when alumina or aluminate particles form at the metal-oxide interface and partially or completely isolate the (Fe,Mn)3O4 layer from the metal. … the overall growth rate of the (Fe,Mn)3O4 layer via outward diffusion of cations is retarded; however, the growth rate of Fe2O3 remains unaltered. The net result is an overall increase in the ratio of the thicknesses of Fe2O3 to (Fe,Mn)3O4.

claim: Continuous outer Mn2O3 layer on small nodules retards ionic diffusion enough to allow formation of a healing Al2O3 or aluminate layer at the nodule base, minimizing further growth.
evidence_text: it is likely that formation of a continuous layer of Mn2O3 at the outer surface may retard the rate of ionic diffusion sufficiently to allow a healing layer of Al2O3 or aluminate to form at the base of the nodule and minimize further growth.

claim: High Mn enrichment on external Fe2O3 of Fe-5Mn-6Al and Fe-7.5Mn-5Al, and presence of a grey Mn2O3 phase as particles at gas-oxide interface and within Fe2O3 layer on Fe-7.5Mn-5Al (Fig. 4).
evidence_text: A high degree of manganese enrichment on the surface of the external Fe2O3 layers on Fe–5Mn–6Al and Fe–7.5Mn–5Al … Closer examination of the scales revealed a grey phase (Mn2O3) present as particles at the gas-oxide interface and within the Fe2O3 layer on the Fe–7.5Mn–5Al alloy (Fig. 4).

claim: At 800°C, Fe–Mn–Al alloys form three nodule types based on Mn content: Type 1 large iron oxide nodules on alloys with <5% Mn; Type 2 large iron oxide nodules on 5–7.5% Mn alloys; Type 3 small manganese rich nodules on alloys containing at least 7.5% Mn.
evidence_text: Fig. 17. Structures of nodules formed on Fe–Mn–Al alloys at 800°C. Type 1: large iron oxide nodules formed on alloys containing less than about 5% manganese. Type 2: large iron oxide nodules formed on alloys containing between about 5% and 7.5% manganese. Type 3: small manganese rich nodules formed on alloys which contained at least 7.5% manganese.

claim: Alloys in region II are mainly duplex, except Fe–30Mn–9Al which has a triplex α + γ + (βMn) structure.
evidence_text: Alloys in this region were mainly duplex, except for Fe–30Mn–9Al, which had a triplex α + γ + (βMn) structure.

claim: Duplex alloys in region II oxidised at 800°C show poor oxidation resistance and two scale morphologies: high ferrite fraction alloys develop alumina with bulky Mn-rich oxides and oxide threads on grain boundaries and specific grain orientations (Fig. 8a), whereas high austenite fraction alloys form bulky Mn-rich oxides and wormlike threads that appear to grow through the alumina (Fig. 10b), often with a secondary ferrite layer at the substrate surface (Fig. 9).
evidence_text: At 800°C alloys from within the two phased region of the oxice map in Fig. 3 generally showed poor oxidation resistance, and the resulting scale morphologies were of two types. Alloys which contained relatively high proportions of ferrite formed alumina with bulky manganese rich oxides and oxide threads (Fig. 10a), which grew above grain boundaries and at distinct orientations on grain surfaces (Fig. 8a). … On alloys which contained higher proportions of austenite, the scale morphologies were generally bulky manganese rich oxides and wormlike oxide threads, which often appeared to have grown through the alumina (Fig. 10b). … and layers of ferrite which formed secondarily were frequently observed at the substrate surface as in Fig. 9.

claim: Secondary ferrite formed by Mn depletion from austenite contains at most the Al level of the austenite; for alloys with <30% Mn this composition lies in region I (non-protective), while for >30% Mn secondary ferrite can reach region III compositions but the alloys contain brittle β-Mn.
evidence_text: In contrast to the primary ferrite, ferrite formed secondarily by depletion of manganese from austenite, contains, at most, the amount of aluminum present in the austenite. For alloys containing less than 30% manganese, this amount is below that required to form protective alumina scales as the composition of the secondary ferrite lies within region I of the oxide map. Alloys which contain more than 30% manganese can form secondary ferrite with compositions in region III of the oxide map; however, they contain significant proportions of the brittle β manganese phase.

claim: The true boundary between protective and nonprotective scales for alloys containing >12% Mn follows the α-(α+γ) phase boundary; oxidation resistant alloys must be fully ferritic, and austenite stabilisers such as carbon are detrimental.
evidence_text: The presence of austenite in alloys with compositions within this region is responsible for their poor oxidation resistance, and thus the true boundary between protective and nonprotective scales follows the α-(α+γ) phase boundary for alloys which contain in excess of about 12% manganese. As resistant alloys must be totally ferritic, carbon and other austenitizing elements are considered to be detrimental to the oxidation resistance unless combined with other elements to prevent the formation of austenite.

claim: Bulky Mn-rich oxide growth in duplex Fe–Mn–Al alloys at 800°C proceeds in five stages: (1) alumina scale on ferrite matrix; (2) austenite plate nucleation beneath alumina; (3) Mn2O3 oxide thread growth at alumina weaknesses; (4) incorporation of threads and alumina into growing bulky oxide with Mn depletion and conversion of austenite to ferrite; (5) continuous healing layer of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4.
evidence_text: Fig. 18. Stages of growth of bulky manganese rich oxides during oxidation of duplex α + γ Fe-Mn-Al alloys at 800°C (PO2 = 200 torr). Stage 1: formation of alumina scale over an initially ferrite matrix. Stage 2: nucleation and growth of austenite plates beneath the alumina scale. Stage 3: Growth of Mn2O3 oxide threads at weaknesses in the overlying alumina scale. Stage 4: incorporation of the oxide threads and alumina scale into the growing bulky manganese rich oxide, involving manganese depletion and conversion of the austenite back to ferrite. Stage 5: formation of a continuous healing layer of Al2O3 or (Fe, Mn)AlO.

claim: In region II, bulky oxide structures depend on Mn rather than Al: alloys with <20% Mn form structures similar to small bulky nodules from region I (≥7.5% Mn); alloys with >20% Mn form highly Mn-enriched oxides lacking Fe2O3, with phases resembling those on binary Fe–40Mn.
evidence_text: The structures of the bulky oxides that formed on alloys in this region appeared to depend upon the concentration of manganese rather than aluminum in the alloy. Alloys which contained less than about 20% manganese had very similar structures to the small bulky oxide nodules which formed on alloys containing at least 7.5% Mn from region I of the oxide map. Alloys with more than approximately 20% manganese formed bulky oxides highly enriched in manganese; these alloys contained no Fe2O3, and the phases appeared similar to the manganese rich phases reported to form on a binary Fe-40Mn alloy.

claim: The scale on Fe–25Mn–7Al oxidised 24 h at 800°C (Fig. 19a) contains Mn2O3, Mn3O4, (Fe,Mn)3O4, and either (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 or Al2O3, with these phases arranged as schematised in Fig. 19b.
evidence_text: Fig. 19. (a) Cross-section showing the oxide phases present in the scale formed on Fe–25Mn–7Al (24 hr, 800°C, Po2=200 torr). (b) Schematic representation of the oxide structures observed on Fe–Mn–Al alloys which contained more than 20% manganese.

claim: Formation of Mn3O4 and (Fe,Mn)3O4 depends on Mn enrichment and growth of (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 at the metal–oxide interface; (Fe,Mn)3O4 is present only where Mn3O4 exists, and its disappearance correlates with continuous or near-continuous aluminate layers at the interface.
evidence_text: Whether formation of the phases Mn3O4 and (Fe,Mn)3O4 does occur appears to depend on the enrichment of manganese and the growth of (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 at the metal-oxide interface. … (Fe,Mn)3O4 was present only in some structures which contained Mn3O4… When complete aluminate layers were not observed, phases of the type Mn3O4 were present… Disappearance of (Fe,Mn)3O4 from the scales was associated with continuous or near-continuous aluminate layers.

claim: In region II at 800°C, weight gains increase with Mn content and decrease with Al content; large weight gain increases above ~10% Mn are directly attributable to the proportion of austenite, and breakaway/healing kinetics are observed, with healing attributed to alumina/aluminate formation reducing cation outward diffusion.
evidence_text: The magnitudes of weight gains within this region of alloys oxidized at 800°C increased with increasing manganese and decreased with increasing aluminum. Since large increases in weight gain are due to growth of oxides other than alumina, that is, bulky manganese rich oxides, the large increases in weight gain observed for alloys which contained more than about 10% manganese are directly attributable to the relative proportion of austenite in the structure. Frequently, breakaway and healing kinetics were observed. … Healing kinetics may be attributed to the formation of alumina or aluminates at the metal-oxide interface, thus reducing the rate of outward diffusion of cations across the scales.

claim: Fe–20Mn–6Al formed a ferrite surface layer during 800°C oxidation and oxidised rapidly with a weight gain exceeding 1 mg after 20 h; the moderate oxidation resistance of austenitic alloys (∼1 mg/day) is attributed to bulky Mn-rich scales and underlying healing layers.
evidence_text: The alloy Fe–20Mn–6Al formed a ferrite surface layer during oxidation at 800°C (Fig. 9) and oxidized rapidly with a weight gain after 20 hr in excess of 1 mg. The moderate oxidation resistance reported for austenitic alloys in the literature, with weight gains typically of the order of 1 mg per day, may be attributed to the formation of bulky manganese rich scales and healing layers of aluminate or alumina beneath.

claim: After 100 h at 800°C, Fe–10Mn–6Al and Fe–20Mn–8Al exhibit scale morphologies similar to 24 h, indicating a stable protective boundary; at 600°C both alloys form bulky Mn-rich oxides requiring higher Al for protection; at 1000°C both are ferritic with convoluted protective alumina scales, showing that the α/(α+γ) and protective boundaries shift to lower Al with increasing temperature.
evidence_text: In addition to oxidation for 24 hr at 800°C, the alloys Fe–10Mn–6Al and Fe–20Mn–8Al were also oxidized for 100 hr at 600, 800, and 1000°C. … At 600°C both alloys formed bulky manganese rich oxides … At 1000°C both alloys were ferritic and formed protective convoluted alumina scales, indicating that the α-(α+γ) phase boundary and the boundary for protective scale formation shifts to lower aluminum concentrations as the temperatures of oxidation increases.

claim: Scales formed on Fe–20Mn–8Al at 1000°C spalled readily on cooling, likely due to large detached areas with few contact points; the two-layered duplex scale consists of an inner alumina layer and an outer Mn3O4–Al2O3 solid solution containing >30 wt% Al2O3 at 1000°C.
evidence_text: Scales formed on Fe–20Mn–8Al at 1000°C spalled readily on cooling, possibly due to large areas of detached scale having few points of contact with the substrate. … The inner layer was considered to be alumina, whilst the outer layer, enriched in manganese and aluminum, was probably a solid solution of Mn3O4 and Al2O3. At temperatures above 950°C, the only oxide of manganese in equilibrium with alumina is Mn3O4, which contains in excess of 30 wt.% Al2O3 at 1000°C.

claim: Scales formed on Fe-20Mn-8Al at 1000°C spalled readily on cooling and consisted of an inner alumina layer and an outer layer, probably a solid solution of Mn3O4 and Al2O3, which contains in excess of 30 wt.% Al2O3 at 1000°C.
evidence_text: the two layered duplex scales formed on both alloys at 1000°C were composed almost entirely of the oxides of manganese and aluminum. The inner layer was considered to be alumina, whilst the outer layer, enriched in manganese and aluminum, was probably a solid solution of Mn3O4 and Al2O3 . At temperatures above 950°C, the only oxide of manganese in equilibrium with alumina is Mn3O4 , which contains in excess of 30 wt.% Al2O3 at 1000°C.

claim: Alloys in Region III were totally ferritic and formed very adherent continuous protective alumina scales without nodules; their 24 h weight gains for alloys containing less than 15% Mn were typically around 0.05 mg cm^{-2}.
evidence_text: Alloys within this region were totally ferritic and were characterized by very adherent continuous protective scales, which were not penetrated by nodules or other bulky oxides. Extremely low 24 hr weight gains, typically of the order of 0.05 mg cm^{-2} for most alloys which contained less than 15% manganese, indicated the formation of alumina scales.

claim: The alloy Fe-15Mn-8Al exhibited a relatively high 24 h weight gain of 0.116 mg cm^{-2}, attributed to a large number of small oxide protrusions covering the entire surface.
evidence_text: The relatively high weight gain for the alloy Fe-15Mn-8Al (0.116 mg cm^{-2}) is considered to result from the large number of small oxide protrusions which covered the entire surface.

claim: Manganese additions are detrimental to oxidation resistance of binary Fe-Al alloys, but for Fe-Al alloys with more than 6% Al, Mn additions were beneficial; alloys containing 5-10% Mn had the lowest weight gain for a given Al level.
evidence_text: From the work of Tomaszewicz and Wallwork, manganese additions were apparently detrimental to the oxidation resistance of binary iron-aluminum alloys. The oxide map in Fig. 3 shows that all alloys in this system which contain less than 6% aluminum lack oxidation resistance and lie in regions I or II. For alloys which contained higher levels of aluminum, the addition of manganese was found to be beneficial, and alloys from region III were highly oxidation resistant. The optimum alloys were considered to contain between 5% and 10% manganese as this concentration resulted in the lowest weight gain for any group of alloys having the same aluminum concentration.

claim: Preferential oxidation of manganese in Fe-Mn-Al alloys can result in initially formed scales of manganese oxides and alumina; the lower oxide dissociation pressure of manganese oxide rich scales favors the transition from internal to external oxidation at lower alloy aluminum concentrations.
evidence_text: it is also possible that preferential oxidation of manganese in Fe-Mn-Al alloys could result in initially formed scales composed of manganese oxides and alumina rather than iron oxides and alumina. The lower oxide dissociation pressure of manganese oxide rich scales at the metal interface would be expected to favor the transition from internal to external oxidation at lower alloy aluminum concentrations.

claim: Region IV alloys formed alumina scales and small manganese-rich oxide nodules penetrating the substrate; EDAX showed nodules on alloys with less than 15% Mn were extremely rich in manganese, probably consisting of higher manganese oxides and (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 at the metal-oxide interface.
evidence_text: Alloys in this region formed alumina scales and small manganese-rich oxide nodules, which penetrated into the substrate. Despite the small size of nodules formed on the surface of alloys containing less than 15% manganese, EDAX analysis showed that they also were extremely rich in manganese. Although their structures were not determined, it is likely that they were composed predominantly of the higher oxides of manganese with (Fe, Mn)Al2O4 at the metal-oxide interface.

claim: Small manganese oxide nodules nucleate at flaws in the alumina scale above substrate voids via reaction of oxygen with Mn vapor; growth proceeds by Mn vapor reaction, outward Mn2+ diffusion through p-type Mn oxides, and inward short-circuit oxygen diffusion filling the void with Mn oxide, followed by healing layer formation of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 that greatly reduces further growth.
evidence_text: Nodule nucleation occurs by reaction of oxygen with manganese vapor at flaws or defects in the alumina scale which lie directly above voids in the substrate. Nodule growth then proceeds almost exclusively by the reaction of oxygen with manganese vapor in the void. This is because the vapor pressure of manganese is many orders of magnitude larger than that of either iron or aluminum. Continued growth is mainly by the outward diffusion of Mn2+ ions since Mn2O3 , Mn3O4 , and MnO are all p-type semiconductors. Inward growth of the nodule occurs by a mechanism involving short-circuit diffusion of oxygen, and “filling in” of the void with manganese oxide rather than iron oxide results. When the void has become filled with manganese oxide, rapid internal oxidation of aluminum in the substrate leads to the formation of a healing layer of Al2O3 or (Fe, Mn) Al2O4 at the nodule-alloy interface, and any further growth occurs at a much reduced rate via diffusion through this layer.

claim: Fe-30Mn-12Al showed numerous smooth rounded voids filled or partially filled with manganese oxide (Fig. 11c), supporting the nodule growth mechanism.
evidence_text: The observation of numerous smooth rounded voids 'filled' or 'partially filled' with manganese oxide, on the surface of Fe-30Mn-12Al (Fig. 11c), supports such a mechanism.

claim: The hollow oxide mounds and convoluted scales on the duplex alloy Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr were almost identical in structure, but the duplex alloy scale was more uneven in thickness (Fig. 16a).
evidence_text: Comparison of the hollow oxide mounds with convoluted scales formed on the duplex alloy Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr revealed almost identical scale structures, except that the scale on the duplex alloy was more uneven in thickness (Fig. 16a).

claim: Bulky oxides formed above austenite plates on Fe-10Mn-4Al-5Cr were highly enriched in manganese, similar to duplex ternary alloys.
evidence_text: Bulky oxides formed above austenite plates on Fe-10Mn-4Al-5Cr were almost certainly highly enriched in manganese as were those formed on duplex ternary alloys.

claim: Convoluted scales on Cr-containing alloys, thicker than alumina and highly enriched in Al, were probably composed of the higher oxides of iron, manganese, and chromium with alumina or aluminate spinel at the scale base.
evidence_text: Their greater thickness than alumina scales and their high degree of enrichment in aluminum suggests that they were composed of the higher oxides of iron, manganese, and chromium with alumina or aluminate spinel at the scale base.

claim: Fe-7.5Mn-4Al-5Cr, which initially lacked austenite, developed oxide threads after 100 h oxidation (Fig. 15b); aluminum depletion from scale formation likely induced austenite due to its composition being extremely close to the α-(α+γ) phase boundary at 800°C.
evidence_text: Growth of manganese oxide threads on Fe-Mn-Al alloys was attributed to the presence of austenite below the alumina scale. Austenite was not present in the structure of Fe-7.5Mn-4Al-5Cr; however, oxide threads were present after 100 hr (Fig. 15b). Aluminum depletion of the substrate due to formation of an alumina scale may have been sufficient to form austenite, as the alloy composition was extremely close to the α-(α+γ) phase boundary at 800°C.

claim: The present oxidation resistant quaternary Fe-Mn-Al-Cr alloys had higher Mn concentrations than those previously reported by Perkins, whose optimal range was Fe-(2.5-5)%Mn-(8-10)%Al-(5-15)%Cr at 982°C, but alloys with <8% Al and 5-20% Mn were not investigated.
evidence_text: Oxidation resistant quaternary alloys contained significantly higher manganese concentrations than similar resistant quaternary alloys reported previously. Perkins found that alloys with compositions within the range Fe-(2.5-5)% Mn-(8-10)% Al-(5-15)% Cr afforded optimum resistance to cyclic oxidation at 1800°F (982°C); however, alloys containing less than 8% aluminum and alloys containing 5-20% manganese were not investigated.

claim: The poor cyclic oxidation resistance of Fe-Mn-Al-Cr alloys with 20-30% Mn was more likely due to repeated growth and spallation of nonadherent convoluted scales, as observed on Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr at 800°C, rather than Mn vaporization.
evidence_text: The poor cyclic oxidation resistance of alloys which contained 20-30% manganese and the associated high weight losses were attributed to vaporization of manganese from the alloy. From the observation of nonadherent highly convoluted scales during isothermal oxidation of Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr at 800°C, it is considered that the high weight losses were more probably due to repeated growth and spallation of the scale during cycling.

claim: Fe-Cr-Al alloys containing 5% chromium require 5% aluminum to form highly protective scales at 800°C.
evidence_text: It has been shown that Fe-Cr-Al alloys containing 5% chromium require 5% aluminum to form highly protective scales at 800°C.

claim: Certain Fe-Mn-Al alloys form alumina scales that are highly protective at 600, 800, and 1000°C.
evidence_text: It has been shown that certain alloys which belong to the iron-manganese-aluminum system oxidize to form alumina scales, which are highly protective at 600, 800, and 1000°C.

claim: An oxide map showing alloy structure and general oxidation behavior as a function of composition has been produced for 800°C.
evidence_text: An oxide map which shows the alloy structure and general oxidation behavior as a function of alloy composition has been produced for the temperature of 800°C.

claim: Totally ferritic alloys in the composition range Fe-(5-10)%Mn-(6-10)%Al were highly resistant to oxidation and worthy of further development.
evidence_text: Totally ferritic alloys with compositions in the range Fe - (5 - 10)% Mn - (6 - 10)% Al were highly resistant to oxidation and were considered worthy of further development.
